summary: DHH launches a 'Facebook-Free Business' campaign, urging companies to completely sever ties with Facebook and its subsidiaries. He argues that Facebook's repeated privacy scandals prove the company can't be trusted with user data. The initiative establishes three clear rules: no advertising on Facebook properties, no using them for business communication or promotion, and no assisting Facebook's data collection through Like buttons or login integrations. Basecamp pledges to go Facebook Free immediately and invites other businesses to join, offering a Creative Commons badge for participants.
key_points: Facebook has demonstrated it cannot be trusted with user data through endless privacy scandals involving selling, bartering, and abusing data
The Facebook-Free Business pledge covers three areas: no ads, no business presence or communication, and no data collection assistance like Like buttons or Facebook logins
Businesses vary in Facebook dependence, so adoption will follow a natural curve from innovators to late majority
Companies don't need to solve the general problem of corporate ethics before taking action against companies that have clearly crossed the line
money_quotes: If Facebook's endless privacy scandals have shown one thing, it's that the company has far too much data on its users, and that they can't be trusted not to sell, barter, or abuse that data whether for profit, growth, or negligence.
Being a Facebook-Free Business means your customers can trust that you aren't collaborators with the Facebook machine.
When you spend your money with a Facebook-Free Business, none of that money will find its way back to Facebook's coffers.
We do not assist Facebook in its data collection regime through use of Facebook social Like buttons or by offering Facebook logins.
We don't have to solve that riddle before we take action against companies that have clearly crossed the line, wherever that may be drawn.
Apologies for the Facebook Like button on this Medium page. We're just about ready to get off Medium as well, so that'll take care of that!
key_insight: Businesses have an ethical obligation to stop funding and enabling Facebook's data exploitation machine, and they should publicly commit to severing all ties with the platform.
